title: Nonparametric Quantile Regression with ReLU-Activated Recurrent Neural Networks
abstract: This paper investigates nonparametric quantile regression using recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and sparse recurrent neural networks (SRNNs) to approximate the conditional quantile function, which is assumed to follow a compositional hierarchical interaction model. We show that RNN- and SRNN-based estimators with rectified linear unit (ReLU) activation and appropriately designed architectures achieve the optimal nonparametric convergence rate, up to a logarithmic factor, under stationary, exponentially $\boldsymbol{\beta}$-mixing processes. To establish this result, we derive sharp approximation error bounds for functions in the hierarchical interaction model using RNNs and SRNNs, exploiting their close connection to sparse feedforward neural networks (SFNNs). Numerical experiments and an empirical study on the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA) further support our theoretical findings.
keywords: nonparametric regression; quantile regression; neural networks; deep learning; non-asymptotic bounds; stationarity

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This (mainly theoretical) contribution studies quantile regression using recurrent neural networks (RNNs). Both fully-connected and sparse RNNs are covered. Generalisation bounds for the empirical risk minimisers are derived under a compositional assumption on the target function. The paper shows advantages of RNNs (sparse and non-sparse) in a simulation study.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strength:
- The paper combines several areas in the literature, including quantile regression, recent approximation results for RNNs, and hierarchical function classes. 
- The mathematics is solid, the paper is well-written, and although this is mainly a theoretical contribution, the paper is accessible to a wider audience. 


Weaknesses:
- There is a close connection between RNNs and FNNs (Proposition 2 in Jiao et al. 24, mentioned on l.78 of the article). This means that approximation theory just carries over. I do not see in the theory why RNNs are really necessary here and why not simply taking an FNN (in the simulations a big difference can be observed)
- Although sparse networks are mentioned as a main contribution, it seems rather straightforward
  

- typo in l.107: $\mathbf{v}_L^{(1)},\ldots, \mathbf{v}_L^{(N)}$ not defined
- can you change $\log n_{\max}/n_{\min}$ to $\log (n_{\max})/n_{\min}$ everywhere?

QUESTIONS:
- provide a more complete literature review (for instance ArXiv:2009.08848 is not cited and discussed)
- work out a theoretical explanation why RNNs are better than FNNs. 
- compare the methods on real data

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies nonparametric quantile regression using recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and sparse recurrent neural networks (SRNNs) to approximate conditional quantile functions with hierarchical interaction structure. The authors demonstrate that RNN and SRNN estimators with ReLU activation achieve optimal nonparametric convergence rates under stationary observations. They derive approximation error bounds for the hierarchical interaction model.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
### Strengths 
- Theoretical results are provided. 

### Weaknesses
- The usefulness of theoretical results are unclear. Theoretical results do not provide any statistical inference methods. 
- The practical applications of the proposed method are not examined in the main text. The paper appeals only to a limited audience and would be more suitable for a specialized journal.
- Please review the literature properly. For instance, one of the important reference is 
   - White, H. (1992). Nonparametric estimation of conditional quantiles using neural networks. In Computing Science and Statistics: Statistics of Many Parameters: Curves, Images, Spatial Models (pp. 190-199). New York, NY: Springer New York.
 - The abstract of the paper mentions an application of GDP forecasting but the main text has no information about it at all. The organization of the paper should be reconsidered. Also, GDP usually do not satisfy the stationary conditions made in the paper and thus the authors might need another application.

QUESTIONS:
- Question 1. How do the convergence rate results translate into practical guidance for model selection, hyperparameter tuning, or confidence interval construction in quantile regression applications?

- Question 2. What methods do the authors propose for hypothesis testing, confidence bands, or uncertainty quantification given their theoretical framework?

- Question  3. Beyond the brief mention in simulations, what detailed empirical evidence demonstrates the practical advantages of RNNs and SRNNs over existing nonparametric quantile regression methods?

- Question  4. How do the authors justify applying their method to GDP forecasting when GDP data typically exhibits non-stationary behavior, violating the stationarity assumptions required by their theory?

- Question  5. Given the disconnect between the abstract's emphasis on GDP forecasting and the main text's purely theoretical focus, how should the authors restructure the paper to better align the promised applications with the delivered content?

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper analyzes the statistical behavior of quantile regression (QR) estimators based on recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and their sparse variants (SRNNs) for time series prediction with dependent data. In contrast to earlier work that often assumes independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) data or short memory processes, this study considers the more general case of stationary $\beta$-mixing sequences, including both exponential and algebraic decay. The authors provide non-asymptotic error bounds and prove oracle inequalities for empirical risk minimization (ERM) using these neural architectures. They also derive minimax-optimal convergence rates (up to logarithmic factors), showing that statistical dependence slows down learning rates in a way that depends on the mixing rate parameter $r$. These results demonstrate that neural QR methods remain effective in dependent settings and extend existing theory on neural network regression.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
### Strengths and Weaknesses

**Strengths**

- **Theoretical novelty under dependence**: The paper extends quantile regression theory to stationary $\beta$-mixing sequences and provides convergence rates that adapt to both exponential and algebraic mixing decay. This represents a meaningful and nontrivial advance beyond existing results for i.i.d. or weakly dependent data.

- **Precise characterization of mixing effects**: The paper clearly shows how the decay exponent $r$ of the $\beta$-mixing coefficients influences learning rates. In particular, algebraic mixing slows the rate by a factor of $(1 - 1/r)$, and the rates gradually approach those for the i.i.d. case as $r \to \infty$.

- **Transparent connection to neural architecture**: The convergence results are explicitly linked to the width and depth of the RNN/SRNN architectures, as well as to sparsity in the SRNN case. These modeling choices are well motivated and consistent with prior work on neural function approximation.

- **Clear comparison with related work**: The paper positions itself clearly with respect to existing literature, emphasizing the novelty of its dependent setting and the use of recurrent network structures.

**Weaknesses**

- **Limited intuition for role of $r$**: Although the dependence of the convergence rate on $r$ is mathematically clear, the paper does not offer much explanation for why this dependence arises. A more detailed discussion of the blocking technique and the trade-off between bias, variance, and dependence would improve understanding.

- **Strong theoretical assumptions**: 
  The paper's theoretical guarantees rely on structural assumptions that may be restrictive or difficult to verify in practice. Specifically:  
  - The target quantile function is assumed to lie in a hierarchical interaction model with known smoothness and sparsity structure ($\mathcal{H}_{d_x \times N}^l(\mathcal{P}, K)$; Assumption 2), which may not hold in real data.  
  - The conditional distribution of the error term must be Lipschitz and uniformly bounded away from zero (Assumption 1), excluding many heavy-tailed or discontinuous noise distributions.  
  - The data-generating process is assumed to be strictly stationary and $\beta$-mixing with known decay, which is difficult to assess empirically and may be violated in real-world time series with regime changes or long memory.

  While such assumptions are common in the theoretical literature, their practical relevance and verifiability should be more thoroughly discussed in the paper.

- **No empirical illustration of theoretical effects**: The paper does not include any experiments showing how convergence or generalization degrades as a function of the mixing rate $r$, or comparing performance between i.i.d. and dependent settings.

- **Restrictive stationarity assumption**:  
  The theoretical results require the sequence $\{\mathbf{z}_t\}$ to be strictly stationary and $\beta$-mixing. While this is a more general dependence structure than assuming i.i.d. samples, the stationarity assumption may still be too strong for many practical time series settings, including financial and macroeconomic data, where trends, regime shifts, or heteroscedasticity are common. The paper does not examine the robustness of the method to nonstationary violations or provide guidance on verifying this assumption empirically.

QUESTIONS:
### Questions and Suggestions for the Authors

- **Origin and intuition of the $(1 - 1/r)$ factor**:  
  The convergence rate under algebraic $\beta$-mixing is slower by a factor of $(1 - 1/r)$ compared to the i.i.d. case. Could you explain where this term comes from in your analysis? Is this slowdown an expected outcome when using standard blocking arguments to handle dependence, or is it specific to your proof technique? A clearer intuition for how the mixing decay parameter $r$ enters the rate would help readers interpret the result.


- **Theoretical role of the blocking parameter $\ell$**:  
  The error bounds rely on a blocking technique with parameter $\ell$ to control dependence under $\beta$-mixing. Could the authors provide more theoretical insight into how $\ell$ should scale with $n$ and the mixing rate $r$ to optimize the trade-off between approximation and dependence errors? Additionally, is the use of blocking sharp or conservative in this context — for example, could a different dependence-handling argument lead to faster rates?


- **Estimating the mixing rate $r$ in practice**:  
  The theoretical results depend on whether the process is exponentially or algebraically mixing, and on the value of $r$. How could $r$ be estimated or checked from real data? If the wrong type of mixing is assumed, how much would that affect model selection or prediction accuracy?

- **Effect of nonstationarity**:  
  The results assume that the process is strictly stationary. However, in real applications, some time series may show slow changes in their distribution. How much do your results rely on the stationarity assumption? Could the method be extended to models that are only locally or piecewise stationary?

- **Effect of stronger dependence (smaller $r$)**:  
  Since the theory suggests that performance depends on $r$, it would be helpful to show a small simulation or example that changes the strength of dependence (e.g., by using different values of $r$). This would make the theoretical results easier to understand for applied researchers.

- **Failure cases**:  
  All examples shown in the paper favor your method. Could you include some experiments where the method might not do well, such as very short time series, weak signals, nonstationary data, or very extreme quantile levels?

- **Computation time and scalability**:  
  It would help to compare the time it takes to train and evaluate each method (QRF, FNN, RNN, SRNN). 

- **Hyperparameter sensitivity**:  
  Neural network methods can be sensitive to their architecture. A small study that shows how performance changes when:
  - the number of layers $L$ is changed,
  - the width $W$ is increased or decreased,
  - the pruning ratio (40%) is changed,  
  would help readers understand how stable the method is and how it should be tuned.

LIMITATIONS:
Yes
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
SUMMARY:
This paper studies nonparametric quantile regression using recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and sparse recurrent neural networks (SRNNs) to approximate the true conditional quantile function, under compositional  model.

STRENGTHS AND WEAKNESSES:
Strengths:
The paper derives nearly optimal rates for ERM estimators based on ReLU RNNs and SRNNs under certain mixing conditions on the data.

Weaknesses:
The main result hinges on bounding both the approximation error and the generalization error. For the generalization error under mixing sequences, the authors rely on existing results. As for the approximation error, the contribution does not meet the publication standards of NeurIPS, as it appears to be a straightforward combination of existing results from Kohler and Langer (2021), Schmidt-Hieber (2020), and Jiao et al. (2024).

QUESTIONS:
Why consider sparse RNNs? What are the benefits of sparse RNN over RNN ?
 For me, a neural network class with sparsity constraint is hard to train in practice.

LIMITATIONS:
This is a theoretical paper; however, the primary approximation results are merely a straightforward combination of existing ones. Consequently, the contribution lacks sufficient novelty.
reviews.TITLE: Official Review

reviews.comments:
DECISION:
Accept (poster)

COMMENT:
This paper studies nonparametric quantile regression using recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and sparse RNNs (SRNNs) to approximate conditional quantile functions with hierarchical compositional structure. The authors establish convergence rates under mixing conditions and derive approximation bounds using connections between sparse feedforward and recurrent architectures. The theoretical results are supported by simulations and an empirical application to GDP forecasting.

While the reviewer team did not reach full consensus, I find the overall contribution to be sound and well-motivated. The theoretical development appears technically solid, and the approach adds value to the literature by bridging quantile regression and recurrent neural architectures under a principled framework.

I recommend acceptance. It would be beneficial for the authors to incorporate feedback from the reviewers and discussion period into the camera-ready version to further improve clarity and positioning.
reviews.TITLE: Decision
